description: Misconception registry for Linguics. A misconception is an ERROR PATTERN, the structured abstraction above a set of surface wrong-forms (capisciamo, finisciamo, preferisciamo all embody one misconception: stem_change.isc_over_extension). Misconceptions are ORTHOGONAL to the skill buckets: one misconception spans many buckets (over-extending a stem change appears in ire_isc, go_verbs, dittongo), and one bucket can be failed by several misconceptions. They are tagged at the point of detection: a grammar item's must_not_include entry (in object form) carries an optional `misconception` field naming an id from this registry; when that wrong form fires, the housing records the usual bucket miss AND a misconception event. Misconception events aggregate on their own axis, surfaced in a SEPARATE drill-down stats view (never inline during questions, so tagging carries no leak risk). The marquee long-term value is the CROSS-KIND families (agreement, accent, regularisation): the same misconception observed across adjectives, participles, articles, pronouns, and verbs. Governance mirrors the glossary and themes registries: architecture-owned and canonical; authors tag against it and propose additions via misconception_suggestions_<topic>.json; architecture ratifies. This v1 seed is populated from the present-indicative formation work plus the general families; it is designed to grow as every topic comes online. Many specifics here can be bootstrapped from the `common_miss` prose attributes already authored across the bucket trees.

agreement.label: Agreement
agreement.description: Wrong gender, number, or person where two elements must agree. The flagship cross-kind family: will be populated by adjectives (rossa/rosso), past participles (andata/andato), articles (il/la), pronouns (lo/la), demonstratives, and subject-verb agreement.

regularisation.label: Regularisation
regularisation.description: Treating an irregular form as if it followed the regular pattern. Cross-kind: irregular verbs, irregular plurals, irregular comparatives.

orthography.label: Orthography (sound-preserving spelling)
orthography.description: Spelling adjustments whose job is to keep the written form consistent with the sound. Cross-kind: verb endings (cerco/cerchi), plural spelling, and similar.

accent_silent_letter.label: Accents and silent letters
accent_silent_letter.description: Written accents and silent letters that are obligatory and often meaning-bearing. Cross-kind: appears anywhere accented or h-bearing forms occur.

stress.label: Word stress
stress.description: Misplaced spoken or written stress. Cross-kind, and links to the proposed word-stress vocabulary dimension (see OPEN_QUESTIONS).

stem_change.label: Stem change (over / under application)
stem_change.description: Over-applying or under-applying a conditioned stem change. Mostly verb morphology, but the over/under-application shape recurs elsewhere.

conjugation_class.label: Conjugation-class confusion
conjugation_class.description: Applying one conjugation class's ending or pattern to a verb of another class.

word_order.label: Word order
word_order.description: Reserved cross-kind family (adjective position, clitic position, etc.). No specifics seeded yet; populated as those topics come online.

register.label: Register
register.description: Reserved cross-kind family (formal/informal mismatch). No specifics seeded yet.

false_friend.label: False friend
false_friend.description: Reserved cross-kind (vocab) family. No specifics seeded yet; populated by the vocab side later.

auxiliary_choice.label: Auxiliary choice
auxiliary_choice.description: Errors in the compound-tense auxiliary: choosing essere vs avere (avere_for_essere, essere_for_avere), and choosing the wrong tense of the auxiliary itself (wrong_auxiliary_tense, e.g. trapassato avevo fatto vs passato prossimo ho fatto). Cross-kind: the same essere/avere split governs passato prossimo, trapassato, futuro anteriore, and the compound subjunctive / conditional.

transfer.label: Transfer (L1 interference)
transfer.description: Errors imported from English or another L1: calques, English-style structures, omissions where English omits. A SECONDARY OVERLAY tag that co-occurs with a structural family rather than replacing it (an English-fronting error is both word_order and transfer). Added 2026-06-09.

paradigm_confusion.label: Paradigm confusion (wrong member selected)
paradigm_confusion.description: Selecting the wrong member of a closed grammatical paradigm where the form produced is itself a valid word but wrong for the role: DOP for IOP (lo for gli), clitic for stressed (mi for me), ci for ne. Distinct from conjugation_class, which is misapplied morphology producing a non-word. Added 2026-06-09.

clitic_cluster.label: Clitic cluster morphophonology
clitic_cluster.description: Errors assembling pronoun clusters: the vowel change (mi + lo to me lo), the glie- fusion (glielo), and joined-when-should-be-separate (melo for me lo). Morphophonological assembly, not sound-preserving spelling (which is orthography). Added 2026-06-09.

discrimination.label: Discrimination (wrong tense / aspect / mood selected)
discrimination.description: Choosing the wrong tense, aspect, or mood among grammatically available options: pp_for_imperfect (the dominant aspect error), modal stative-vs-event aspect, progressive overuse, future/present-for-future mismatch, counterfactual construction, trapassato-vs-imperfect, sequence of tenses, indicative-for-subjunctive CHOICE (the trigger demanded the subjunctive). The selection counterpart to the formation families; fires from the .discrimination bucket branches and tense_choice. Includes a flagged recognition-direction specific (narrative_imperfect_reading) that is comprehension, not production. Added 2026-06-09.

mood.label: Mood (wrong mood form built)
mood.description: Producing the wrong mood's FORM where another mood was required: indicative_for_subjunctive_form (built the indicative where the subjunctive was needed, in congiuntivo and the formal imperative), subjunctive over-application, conditional/subjunctive form confusion. Distinct from the SELECTION error (discrimination.indicative_for_subjunctive_choice = didn't know the trigger demanded it) and from paradigm_confusion (wrong member of a closed paradigm). Added 2026-06-09.

gemination.label: Gemination (double consonant)
gemination.description: A phonemic geminate written single (or vice versa): geminate_simplified (vorrò to vorò, parlassi to parlasi, parlammo to parlamo) and enclitic_doubling (dammi to dami). A sound error, not sound-preserving spelling (which is orthography), hence its own family. Recurs across verbs, enclitics, and vocab. Added 2026-06-09.

misconceptions.label: Written accent omitted
misconceptions.description: Omitting an obligatory written accent, often producing a different word (e for è) or an unmarked vowel (puo for può, da for dà). Strongly cross-cutting.

misconceptions.label: Silent h dropped on avere
misconceptions.description: Dropping the obligatory silent h on the present forms of avere, producing a different real word (o, ai, a, anno).

misconceptions.label: Adjective fails to agree with its noun
misconceptions.description: Leaving an adjective in the wrong gender or number for the noun it modifies, where the adjective is of the right inflection class.

misconceptions.label: Auxiliary in the wrong person/number for the subject
misconceptions.description: Conjugating the compound-tense auxiliary for the wrong person or number relative to the subject (subject-verb agreement failure on the auxiliary).
examples: Marco hanno parlato (for Marco ha parlato)

misconceptions.label: Object clitic in the wrong gender or number
misconceptions.description: Choosing a clitic of the wrong gender/number for its referent, where function (DOP/IOP) is correct.
examples: la (for le, gender slip on an IOP)

misconceptions.label: Mixed-gender plural not defaulted to masculine
misconceptions.description: With a mixed-gender plural group, agreeing with the nearest noun instead of using the masculine-plural default.
examples: i ragazzi e le ragazze sono italiane (for italiani)

misconceptions.label: essere-participle fails to agree with the subject
misconceptions.description: With essere as auxiliary, leaving the participle in the masculine-singular default instead of agreeing in gender and number with the subject.
examples: sono andati (for a feminine group, sono andate)

misconceptions.label: avere-participle wrongly agreed with the subject
misconceptions.description: Agreeing the participle with the subject when avere is the auxiliary and there is no preceding DOP, where it must stay invariable. The over-application (hyper-correction) error.
examples: ho mangiata (for ho mangiato, female speaker)

misconceptions.label: avere-participle fails to agree with a preceding direct-object clitic
misconceptions.description: With avere as auxiliary and a preceding 3rd-person DOP (lo/la/li/le) or partitive ne, leaving the participle invariable instead of agreeing with that object.
examples: le ho visti / le ho visto (for le ho viste)
ne ho mangiato tre (for ne ho mangiate tre)

misconceptions.label: Wrong allomorph of a pre-nominal form (bello/buono/quello)
misconceptions.description: Selecting the wrong allomorph of an adjective that inflects like an article before the noun, choosing by the following sound.

misconceptions.label: Reflexive pronoun wrong for the subject
misconceptions.description: Using a reflexive pronoun that doesn't agree with the subject's person (mi lavi for ti lavi). A subject-agreement error; the first foothold of the agreement family on the verb side.

misconceptions.label: avere used where essere is required
misconceptions.description: Selecting avere as the compound-tense auxiliary for a verb that takes essere: motion/change intransitives, reflexives, and the piacere family.
examples: Mi ho lavato (for mi sono lavato)
Le ha piaciuto (for le è piaciuto)

misconceptions.label: essere used where avere is required
misconceptions.description: Selecting essere for a transitive or avere-taking verb, the rarer reverse of the default-avere error, often a hyper-correction.
examples: sono mangiato la pasta (for ho mangiato la pasta)

misconceptions.label: Wrong tense of the auxiliary in a compound tense
misconceptions.description: Using the wrong tense of the (correct) auxiliary: the present 'ho/sono' where the trapassato needs the imperfect 'avevo/ero', or a different past where another compound tense needs its specific auxiliary tense.

misconceptions.label: Clitic cluster fails its obligatory form change
misconceptions.description: Not applying the obligatory changes when clitics combine: mi/ti/ci/vi to me/te/ce/ve before lo/la/li/le/ne, and gli/le + lo/la to glielo/gliela (written attached).
examples: mi lo dai (for me lo dai)
Mi le lavo (for me le lavo)
gli lo dico / le lo dico (for glielo dico)

misconceptions.label: -ano 3pl on an -ere/-ire verb
misconceptions.description: Using the -are 3pl ending -ano on an -ere or -ire verb, which takes -ono.

misconceptions.label: Wrong-class gerund ending (-ando/-endo)
misconceptions.description: Using -endo on an -are verb or -ando on an -ere/-ire verb (and the malformed -iendo).

misconceptions.label: -ono 3pl on an -are verb
misconceptions.description: Using the -ere/-ire 3pl ending -ono on an -are verb, which takes -ano.

misconceptions.label: Another class's theme vowel borrowed
misconceptions.description: Using one conjugation class's theme vowel/ending on a verb of another class (beyond the existing 3pl -ano/-ono specifics).
examples: prendi (the -are subjunctive ending on an -ere verb)
parlessi (the -ere imperfect-subjunctive vowel on an -are verb, for parlassi)

misconceptions.label: Background and foreground tenses swapped
misconceptions.description: Reversing the imperfect-background / passato-prossimo-event pairing, putting the completed event in the imperfect and the ongoing frame in the passato prossimo.
examples: ho dormito mentre bussava (for dormivo mentre ha bussato / bussava)

misconceptions.label: Wrong tense/mood combination for a counterfactual
misconceptions.description: Mis-selecting the verb forms of a counterfactual conditional: using present indicative + future (a real-condition pattern) for a counterfactual, or judging the accepted colloquial double-imperfect as wrong.
examples: se ho tempo, verro' (real-condition pattern) for a counterfactual
treating the colloquial imperfect counterfactual as an error

misconceptions.label: Future vs present-for-future mismatch
misconceptions.description: Choosing the morphological future where a present-for-future is natural, or the present where the future is required (distant, uncertain, probabilistic).
examples: morphological future where present-for-future is more natural

misconceptions.label: Indicative chosen where a trigger requires the subjunctive
misconceptions.description: Leaving the verb in the indicative after a trigger that requires the subjunctive (opinion, emotion, hypothetical conjunctions, negation). The SELECTION error, distinct from mis-forming the subjunctive (see mood.indicative_for_subjunctive_form).
examples: penso che e' (for penso che sia)
sono contento che sei qui (for che tu sia qui)

misconceptions.label: Modal/stative verb: wrong past tense for the intended reading
misconceptions.description: Missing the meaning shift that aspect forces on modal and stative verbs (sapere, dovere, potere, volere, conoscere): the imperfect = ongoing state/ability, the passato prossimo = a bounded event (found out, managed, insisted, met).
examples: imperfect sapevo for the event 'seppi/ho saputo' (found out)

misconceptions.label: Narrative imperfect misread as habitual
misconceptions.description: On the comprehension side, reading a single-event narrative imperfect as if it were habitual/repeated.
examples: reading 'moriva' (narrative) as 'used to die' rather than the single dramatic event

misconceptions.label: Passato prossimo used where the imperfect's aspect is required
misconceptions.description: Choosing the passato prossimo for a context the imperfect owns: habitual action, ongoing state/description, duration, and age/time/weather states. The dominant aspect error of the past tenses.
examples: e' stata stanca for era stanca (state)
ho aspettato da venti minuti for aspettavo
ho avuto dieci anni / ha fatto freddo for avevo/faceva

misconceptions.label: Progressive over-used for the simple tense
misconceptions.description: Using the stare + gerundio progressive where the simple present/imperfect is the natural choice, typically by mapping every English -ing form onto a progressive.
examples: sto mangiando for the habitual mangio
stavo mangiando for the descriptive mangiavo

misconceptions.label: Embedded-clause tense not back-shifted in reported speech
misconceptions.description: Failing to back-shift the embedded clause after a past reporting verb: leaving the present, or using the future where the conditional (future-in-the-past) is required.
examples: ha detto che e' stanco (for che era stanco)
ha detto che andra' (for che sarebbe andato)

misconceptions.label: Trapassato vs imperfect mis-selected
misconceptions.description: Using the imperfect for a clearly completed prior-to-prior event, or the trapassato for an ongoing background state.
examples: avevo mangiato quando sei entrato (implies finished) where ongoing was meant

misconceptions.label: Consonant not doubled on a monosyllabic imperative + clitic
misconceptions.description: Failing to double the initial consonant of a clitic attached to a monosyllabic tu imperative (dammi, dimmelo, fammelo).

misconceptions.label: Obligatory double consonant written single
misconceptions.description: Writing a single consonant where Italian has a phonemic geminate: the assimilated -rr- of syncopated futures/conditionals, the -ss- of the imperfect subjunctive, the -mm-/-mm of passato-remoto/conditional 1pl.

misconceptions.label: Indicative form built where the subjunctive is required
misconceptions.description: Producing the indicative ending/auxiliary when the subjunctive is called for: -ano for -ino, indicative 3pl for the subjunctive 3pl, present/imperfect indicative auxiliary for the subjunctive one.

misconceptions.label: Wrong -chi/-ci (or -ghi/-gi) by stress or irregularity
misconceptions.description: Misapplying the -co plural split (-chi hard vs -ci soft, by stress and lexical irregularity): over-applying one where the other is required.

misconceptions.label: Redundant i kept on -ciare/-giare
misconceptions.description: Keeping a stem i before an ending that already begins with i, producing a wrong double i. Appears in -ciare/-giare verb endings (cominci, not *cominci-i) and in -cia/-gia noun and adjective plurals (grigie/grige, camicie).

misconceptions.label: Clitic not elided before a vowel
misconceptions.description: Failing to elide lo/la to l' before a vowel, or eliding wrongly.

misconceptions.label: Wrong i-retention on -iare by stress
misconceptions.description: Mishandling the stress-governed i of -iare verbs: keeping it when unstressed (studii) or dropping it when stressed (invi).

misconceptions.label: h omitted on -care/-gare
misconceptions.description: Dropping the h that keeps c/g hard before a front vowel, changing the consonant sound. Appears in -care/-gare verb endings (cerchi, paghi) and in -co/-go noun and adjective plurals (bianchi, lunghi).

misconceptions.label: ci and ne confused (a- vs di-)
misconceptions.description: Choosing ci for a di-referent or ne for an a-referent, mis-mapping the preposition the clitic stands in for.

misconceptions.label: Clitic and stressed pronoun confused
misconceptions.description: Using a stressed pronoun where the unmarked clitic is wanted, or a clitic where emphasis needs the stressed form; also article/subject pronoun for an object clitic.
examples: Marco vede me (for Marco mi vede)
Mi piace (where 'a me piace' emphasis is wanted)

misconceptions.label: Direct- and indirect-object clitic confused
misconceptions.description: Using a DOP where an IOP is required or vice versa (li for gli): a real word, wrong role.
examples: li ho detto (for gli ho detto)

misconceptions.label: Future 1pl written for the conditional 1pl
misconceptions.description: Writing the single-m future 1pl (-emo) where the double-m conditional (-emmo) is required, the two differing only by the geminate.

misconceptions.label: Wrong present-tense cell used as the imperative
misconceptions.description: Using a valid present-indicative person as the tu imperative: the 2sg -i for the -are imperative (which is the 3sg -a), or the wrong vowel on -ere/-ire.

misconceptions.label: tu and Lei forms mixed
misconceptions.description: Mixing the informal tu system with the formal Lei system: an informal clitic for the formal La, or an informal imperative where the formal (subjunctive-based) one is required.
examples: ti ringrazio, signor Bianchi (for La ringrazio)
Non mi parla (register slip in a negative imperative)

misconceptions.label: Class II (-e) adjective inflected as Class I (-o/-a)
misconceptions.description: Treating an -e-class adjective (grande) as if it took the dominant -o/-a Class I pattern.

misconceptions.label: Invariable adjective wrongly inflected
misconceptions.description: Inflecting an adjective that should stay invariable: noun-derived colours (rosa, viola) and compound colour expressions (verde scuro).
examples: le pareti verdi scure / verde scure (for verde scuro)

misconceptions.label: Irregular past participle regularised
misconceptions.description: Forming an irregular past participle by the regular -ato/-uto/-ito rule instead of the suppletive/contracted form.

misconceptions.label: Irregular verb regularised
misconceptions.description: Conjugating an irregular verb as if it were regular, ignoring the suppletive or contracted paradigm. Recurs in every tense.
unreachable_notes: imperativo irregular_tu: norm() folds the apostrophe, so a regularised bare 'va' scores as correct va' and no guard can catch it. Empty imperativo stats here do NOT mean learners never regularise apocopated forms - they may do it constantly at full marks. (2026-07-15)

misconceptions.label: Diphthong kept in noi/voi
misconceptions.description: Keeping the stressed-stem diphthong in the 1pl/2pl, where stress moves to the ending and the plain stem returns.

misconceptions.label: Diphthong lost in the singular
misconceptions.description: Failing to diphthongise the stressed stem vowel in the singular or 3pl.

misconceptions.label: -are→-er future/conditional stem shift mishandled
misconceptions.description: Failing to shift the -are theme vowel to -er- in the future/conditional stem, or over-applying that shift to verbs (dare/stare/fare) that keep -ar-.

misconceptions.label: Future/conditional stem over-syncopated
misconceptions.description: Syncopating a regular stem by false analogy with the irregular syncopated futures.

misconceptions.label: Irregular future/conditional stem not syncopated
misconceptions.description: Keeping the theme vowel where the irregular future/conditional stem syncopates (avr-, andr-, potr-).

misconceptions.label: -g- inserted in the wrong person
misconceptions.description: Carrying the inserted -g- of a -go verb into a person that should not have it (the 1pl/2pl, or a regular-stem form).

misconceptions.label: -g- omitted where required
misconceptions.description: Failing to insert the -g- in the 1sg/3pl of a -go verb.

misconceptions.label: -isc- added in noi/voi
misconceptions.description: Over-extending the -isc- infix beyond where it belongs: into the present (and present-subjunctive) 1pl/2pl where it never appears, or into tenses built on the plain stem (future/conditional finirò not *finiscerò, gerund finendo not *finiscendo). The infix is present-system only.

misconceptions.label: -isc- omitted where required
misconceptions.description: Failing to insert -isc- in the singular or 3pl of an -isc- verb.

misconceptions.label: Strong passato-remoto stem used in the weak persons
misconceptions.description: Carrying the strong (rhizotonic) stem into the 2sg/1pl/2pl, which take the regular weak stem and endings.

misconceptions.label: 3pl stressed on the penult
misconceptions.description: Misplacing the 3pl stress onto the penult (parlAno) instead of the antepenult (pArlano). Prosodic; links to the proposed word-stress vocab dimension.

misconceptions.label: 1pl/2pl stress misplaced
misconceptions.description: Mis-stressing the 1pl/2pl, where the stress falls on the ending, by analogy with the stem-stressed singular.

misconceptions.label: English argument structure calqued
misconceptions.description: Mapping English subject/object roles or prepositions onto Italian, especially experiencer verbs (piacere) and overly literal prepositional phrases.
examples: Io piaccio la pizza (for Mi piace la pizza)

misconceptions.label: Required clitic omitted (English has no equivalent)
misconceptions.description: Failing to use a clitic Italian requires because English expresses the meaning without one: partitive/pronominal ne, locative/topic/existential ci, neuter lo, lexicalised clusters (farcela).
examples: E' un problema (for C'e' un problema)
Non lo faccio (for Non ce la faccio)

misconceptions.label: Infinitive used where a non-finite Italian form is required
misconceptions.description: Calquing an English structure by pairing stare with an infinitive instead of a gerund (the progressive), an L1-driven construction error.

misconceptions.label: Finite form negated instead of non + infinitive
misconceptions.description: Negating the positive tu imperative directly (English 'don't speak') instead of the Italian non + infinitive.

misconceptions.label: Adjective placed before the noun where post-nominal is default
misconceptions.description: Fronting a descriptive adjective on the English pattern, where Italian's unmarked order is post-nominal.
examples: la rossa casa (for la casa rossa)

misconceptions.label: Adverb or negation misplaced around aux + participle
misconceptions.description: Breaking the fixed aux-then-participle block: putting negation or a frequency/time adverb (già, ancora, mai, sempre, appena) outside the slot between auxiliary and participle.
examples: ho non mangiato (for non ho mangiato)
ho mangiato già (for ho già mangiato)

misconceptions.label: Clitic cluster in the wrong internal order
misconceptions.description: Ordering a two-clitic cluster wrongly, chiefly DOP before IOP.
examples: Lo mi dai? (for Me lo dai?)

misconceptions.label: Clitic in the wrong position relative to the verb
misconceptions.description: Misplacing a clitic: after a finite verb, detached from an infinitive/gerund/imperative that requires attachment, or attached to a formal Lei imperative where it must precede.

misconceptions.label: -cia/-gia plural i kept or dropped wrongly
misconceptions.description: The noun-plural i-retention split: vowel before -cia/-gia keeps the i (ciliegie, valigie, camicie), consonant before drops it (arance, facce, piogge). Misses in both directions: arancie, camice.
